---
title: "Pueraria Tuberosa"
slug: pueraria-tuberosa
category: "Fruits, Veg & Botanicals"
category-slug: fruits-veg-botanicals
keywords: ["Pueraria Tuberosa", "Vidarikand", "Ayurvedic herb", "Rasayana", "Tuber", "Adaptogen", "Traditional medicine"]
description: "Pueraria tuberosa, known as Vidarikand, is a significant perennial climbing legume native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia"
---

## History and Sourcing

Pueraria tuberosa, known as Vidarikand, is a significant perennial climbing legume native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. Its Sanskrit name, 'earth-splitting tuber,' reflects its deep roots. Revered in Ayurveda as a rasayana (rejuvenative tonic) and adaptogen, it promotes vitality. It is sourced from dry deciduous forests in Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, and the Western Ghats, growing wild and cultivated for medicinal use.

## Home Kitchen Use

Primarily medicinal, Pueraria tuberosa tubers have limited culinary use in traditional Indian rural and tribal communities. The starchy, slightly sweet root is consumed as a famine food (boiled, roasted, or ground into flour for porridges/flatbreads). It is a key ingredient in Ayurvedic formulations like Chyawanprash and is sometimes added to health drinks or gruels for nourishment and strength.

## Industrial Applications

Industrially, Pueraria tuberosa is used in nutraceuticals and herbal supplements. Extracts and powdered root are found in health drinks, energy bars, and Ayurvedic formulations, valued for adaptogenic, immunomodulatory, and anabolic properties. Its isoflavone content, particularly puerarin, is of scientific interest for health management, driving demand for standardized extracts in the wellness industry.

## Distinctions and Common Confusion

Confusion often arises because 'Vidarikand' is also used for Ipomoea digitata (Ksheer Vidari). While both are medicinal, Pueraria tuberosa is botanically distinct with a unique phytochemical profile. Pueraria tuberosa has larger, more fibrous tubers and different leaf morphology compared to Ipomoea digitata's palmate leaves and smaller, succulent tubers. Correct identification is vital for proper traditional medicinal application.
